content:
# Genesis Chapter 42 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25r–25v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_042_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 42–43 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0046

---

## Jacob Sends His Sons to Egypt

**1** And Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt, and Jacob said to his sons, "Why do you look at one another?"

**2** And he said, "Behold, I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy for us from there, so that we may live and not die."

**3** And Joseph's ten brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.

**4** But Benjamin, Joseph's brother, Jacob did not send with his brothers, for he said, "Lest harm befall him."

**5** And the sons of Israel came to buy among those who came, for the famine was in the land of Canaan.

## The Brothers Bow Before Joseph

**6** Now Joseph was the ruler over the land — he was the one who sold to all the people of the land. And Joseph's brothers came and bowed down to him with their faces to the ground.

**7** And Joseph saw his brothers and recognized them, but he made himself unrecognizable to them and spoke harshly with them and said to them, "From where do you come?" And they said, "From the land of Canaan, to buy food."

**8** And Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him.

**9** And Joseph remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them, and he said to them, "You are spies — to see the nakedness of the land you have come."

## The Accusation of Espionage

**10** And they said to him, "No, my lord — your servants have come to buy food.

**11** We are all sons of one man; we are honest men — your servants have never been spies."

**12** And he said to them, "No — you have come to see the nakedness of the land."

**13** And they said, "Your servants are twelve brothers, sons of one man in the land of Canaan. And behold, the youngest is with our father today, and the one is no more."

**14** And Joseph said to them, "It is as I spoke to you, saying: you are spies.

**15** By this you shall be tested: by the life of Pharaoh, you shall not go out from here unless your youngest brother comes here.

**16** Send one of you and let him fetch your brother, and you shall be bound, and your words shall be tested — whether there is truth in you. And if not, by the life of Pharaoh, you are indeed spies."

**17** And he gathered them into custody for three days.

## The Test and the Brothers' First Confession

**18** And Joseph said to them on the third day, "Do this and live — I fear God.

**19** If you are honest men, let one of your brothers be bound in your prison-house, and you — go, bring grain for the famine in your houses.

**20** And bring your youngest brother to me, and your words shall be confirmed, and you shall not die." And they did so.

**21** And they said to one another, "Truly we are guilty concerning our brother, for we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us and we did not listen — therefore this distress has come upon us."

**22** And Reuben answered them and said, "Did I not say to you, 'Do not sin against the boy'? But you did not listen. And now, behold — his blood is being required."

**23** And they did not know that Joseph was listening, for the interpreter was between them.

**24** And he turned away from them and wept. Then he returned to them and spoke to them, and he took Simeon from them and bound him before their eyes.

## The Return Home: Money in the Sacks

**25** And Joseph commanded that they fill their vessels with grain and restore each man's silver to his sack, and to give them provisions for the road. And so it was done for them.

**26** And they loaded their donkeys with their grain and went from there.

**27** And the one opened his sack to give fodder to his donkey at the lodging place, and he saw his silver — and behold, it was in the mouth of his bag.

**28** And he said to his brothers, "My silver has been returned, and also — behold, it is in my bag!" And their hearts went out, and they turned trembling to one another, saying, "What is this that God has done to us?"

## The Brothers' Report to Jacob

**29** And they came to Jacob their father, to the land of Canaan, and they told him all that had happened to them, saying:

**30** "The man, the lord of the land, spoke harshly with us and took us for spies of the land.

**31** And we said to him: We are honest men — we have never been spies.

**32** We are twelve brothers, sons of our father — the one is no more, and the youngest is with our father today in the land of Canaan.

**33** And the man, the lord of the land, said to us: By this I shall know that you are honest men — leave one of your brothers with me, and take grain for the famine of your houses and go.

**34** And bring your youngest brother to me, and I shall know that you are not spies but honest men. I will give your brother to you, and you may trade in the land."

## The Silver Discovered — Jacob's Lament

**35** And it came to pass as they were emptying their sacks that behold, each man's bundle of silver was in his sack. And they and their father saw the bundles of silver, and they were afraid.

**36** And Jacob their father said to them, "You have bereaved me — Joseph is no more, and Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin! Upon me have all these things come."

**37** And Reuben spoke to his father, saying, "Put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back to you — give him into my hand and I will return him to you."

**38** And he said, "My son shall not go down with you, for his brother is dead and he alone is left. If harm should befall him on the road on which you go, then you will bring down my gray head in sorrow to Sheol."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of two Leningrad Codex manuscript images and two Vaticanus manuscript images.

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 42 spans Folios 25r–25v across two manuscript images.

   **Folio 25r** (GEN_042_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 48, the recto of Folio 25. The three-column layout is clearly legible in the West Semitic Research Project photograph with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation throughout. The folio contains the conclusion of Genesis 41 (vv. 53–57) in the opening column, followed immediately by the beginning of Genesis 42. The parashah break marking the transition from Genesis 41 to 42 is visible, after which the folio carries Genesis 42 through approximately verse 20. The opening verse of the chapter — וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב כִּי יֶשׁ שֶׁבֶר בְּמִצְרָיִם (vayyar Ya'akov ki yesh shever b'Mitsrayim — "and Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt") — is clearly legible. The dramatic central section is legible throughout: the command to go down (vv. 1–2), the ten brothers' descent (v. 3), the explicit exclusion of Benjamin (v. 4), and the arrival in Egypt (v. 5). The pivotal prostration scene — וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה (vayyishtachavu lo appayim artsah — "and they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground," v. 6) — is legible with full vocalization, as is the key phrase וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם (vayyitnakker alehem — "and he made himself unrecognizable to them," v. 7). The accusation of espionage (vv. 9–17) and the confinement for three days, then the revised test (vv. 18–20) are legible through the end of this column section.

   **Folio 25v** (GEN_042_page_002.jpg): This is PDF page 49, the verso of Folio 25, photographed in inverted orientation. The three-column layout is fully legible. The folio carries Genesis 42 from approximately verse 21 through verse 38, followed by the beginning of Genesis 43. The brothers' confession — אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ עַל אָחִינוּ (ashemin anachnu al achinu — "we are guilty concerning our brother," v. 21) — is clearly legible, as is Reuben's rebuking speech (v. 22), Joseph's weeping (v. 24), the binding of Simeon (v. 24), the restored silver (vv. 25–28), and the brothers' return to Jacob (vv. 29–34). The climactic Jacob-lament (vv. 35–38) closes the chapter, ending with the Sheol reference: לְהוֹרִיד שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאוֹלָה (l'horid seyvati b'yagon she'olah — "you will bring down my gray head in sorrow to Sheol," v. 38).

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 42** (GEN_042_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 42 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0046). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 41 and the beginning of Genesis 42. The continuous uncial script shows the chapter boundary for Genesis 42. The page carries the chapter from the opening (Jacob sends his sons, vv. 1–5) through the brothers' arrival, the prostration before Joseph (v. 6), and into the interrogation and espionage accusation (vv. 7–20). The key LXX rendering of Joseph's self-concealment — and the Greek equivalent of vayyitnakker — is legible in this portion of the page.

   **Page 43** (GEN_042_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 43 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0047). The chapter notation "CAP XLVIII" is visible in the upper margin, identifying the Vaticanus chapter numbering for this section. This page carries the brothers' confession (v. 21), Reuben's speech (v. 22), Joseph's weeping (v. 24), Simeon's binding (v. 24), the return of the money in the sacks (vv. 25–28), the report to Jacob (vv. 29–34), and Jacob's lament concluding with the Sheol reference (v. 38). The page also carries the opening of Genesis 43.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Grain" — שֶׁבֶר (shever) (vv. 1, 2, 3, 5, 19, 25, 26)**

The Hebrew word rendered "grain" throughout most of Genesis 42 is שֶׁבֶר (shever) rather than the more common דָּגָן (dagan) or בָּר (bar). The root שׁ-ב-ר (sh-b-r) means "to break" in its verbal form — the basic sense is "broken/cracked grain," grain that has been threshed and broken from the husk, ready for use. By extension, shever becomes the term for grain as a marketable commodity or food supply — "provisions, rations, grain for purchase." The cognate Aramaic shibbera and Akkadian šibrum carry similar meanings. Shever is the commercial term: the grain one buys in the marketplace when the food supply is in crisis.

The deployment of shever rather than dagan or bar is significant: it marks the transactions of the chapter as marketplace exchanges, grain purchased under crisis conditions, not the agricultural plenty of the years of abundance. The verb בָּר (bar — "grain") appears in the parallel context of Genesis 41:35 (the gathering of the abundance), while shever dominates Genesis 42–44 (the brothers' purchasing trips). The formal translation renders shever as "grain" throughout, noting this distinct commercial register where relevant.

The LXX renders shever as σῖτος (sitos — "grain, wheat, provisions") — the standard Greek grain term, without the "broken grain" etymology. The formal translation follows the MT's term while rendering in natural English.

**2. "They bowed down to him with their faces to the ground" — וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה (vayyishtachavu lo appayim artsah) (v. 6)**

The central act of verse 6 — the ten brothers prostrating before Joseph — is the first partial fulfillment of Joseph's first dream (37:7: "behold, your sheaves arose and also stood upright, and behold, my sheaf arose and stood upright, and your sheaves came around and bowed down to my sheaf"). The exact language of prostration here deserves close analysis.

The verb וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ (vayyishtachavu) is the Hishtaphel/Hithpael of שׁ-ח-ה (sh-ch-h — "to bow, to prostrate oneself"), the standard term for full-body prostration before a superior: face to the ground, the most complete form of obeisance. This is not merely a respectful nod but the full royal/divine prostration — the same word used when Abraham bowed before the three visitors (18:2), and Lot before the angels (19:1), and Abraham's servant before the LORD (24:26, 48). In the royal-court context of verse 6, it is the subject's prostration before the ruler.

The phrase אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה (appayim artsah) — "faces to the ground" — is the visual specification of the act: the dual form אַפַּיִם (appayim — "faces, nostrils," dual of אַף, literally "the two nostrils/the face") pressed אָרְצָה (artsah — "to the ground, groundward"). The earthward direction of the nostrils/face is the complete gesture of submission.

The phrase vayyishtachavu lo appayim artsah is an exact verbal echo of the dream's content in Genesis 37:7 and 37:9. The narrator does not need to announce "the dream was fulfilled" — the language itself carries that weight. Every reader who has followed the narrative from chapter 37 will recognize the echo.

Joseph's reaction is immediate and pointed: verse 9 says "Joseph remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them." The prostration triggers his memory of the dreams. He does not celebrate or reveal himself; instead the recognition activates the elaborate test that will drive the remainder of the chapter. Why? The second dream (37:9) included eleven stars — all eleven brothers including Benjamin. Only ten are present. The first dream is fulfilled but the second is not yet: the test Joseph devises will bring all eleven brothers, including Benjamin, before him. The fulfillment must be complete.

The LXX renders vayyishtachavu as προσεκύνησαν (prosekunesan — "they did obeisance, they worshipped/prostrated"), its standard rendering of the Hishtaphel throughout Genesis. The term carries the full weight of royal/divine prostration in Greek, which is why the same word is used in the LXX and NT for both royal homage and divine worship.

**3. "He made himself unrecognizable" — וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם (vayyitnakker alehem) (v. 7)**

The verb וַיִּתְנַכֵּר (vayyitnakker) is the Hithpael of נ-כ-ר (n-k-r — "to recognize, to acknowledge"), here with a reflexive/factitive force: "he caused himself to be unrecognized, he made himself strange to them, he acted unrecognizably." The root נ-כ-ר appears in this chapter as a semantic cluster, and its reach across the Joseph narrative is significant.

In Genesis 37:32, the brothers ask their father to "please recognize" (hakker-na) the bloody coat — the same root n-k-r used to force recognition of a deceptive token. In Genesis 38:25, Tamar says to Judah, "Please recognize (hakker-na) whose these are" — forcing recognition of the signet, cord, and staff in the moment of Judah's exposure. The root carries the associations of recognition forced at moments of moral crisis or reversal.

Now in 42:7, Joseph wields the root reflexively: he is the one who controls whether he will be recognized. Where the brothers in chapter 37 and Judah in chapter 38 were forced into recognition by others, Joseph here chooses not to be recognized — he holds recognition in reserve as a narrative and moral tool. The withholding of recognition is the engine of the entire chapter: only when Joseph chooses, at the right moment, will he make himself known (yitgad — 45:1). Until then, he uses the non-recognition to orchestrate the brothers' movement toward repentance.

The Hithpael vayyitnakker also carries a connotation of "acting strangely, acting foreign" — Joseph behaves as a stranger to his brothers, speaks to them through an interpreter (v. 23), presents himself as an Egyptian official entirely alien to them. This is a kind of performance: the man who is their brother acts the role of a foreign ruler entirely. The root's reflexive dimension emphasizes the performative character of the disguise — this is something Joseph deliberately enacts, not a natural condition.

The formal translation renders "he made himself unrecognizable" — preserving the causative-reflexive force of the Hithpael and the choice that lies behind the act. Some translations render "he treated them as strangers" — this captures the social dimension but loses the concealment dimension. The formal translation holds both: the making-strange and the non-recognition.

**4. "You are spies" — מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם (meraglim attem) (vv. 9, 14)**

The accusation Joseph levels against his brothers — מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם ("you are spies") — is the accusation that will drive the entire test and shape the brothers' movements through chapters 42–44. The word מְרַגְּלִים (meraglim) derives from the root ר-ג-ל (r-g-l — "foot, to walk, to travel on foot"), with the Piel participle meaning "those who go about on foot to spy, spies, scouts." It is the same word used for the twelve spies Moses sends into Canaan in Numbers 13 and the two spies Joshua sends to Jericho in Joshua 2:1.

The accusation has a particular charge in the context of the seven-year famine: Egypt is the granary of the ancient world, and foreign visitors buying large quantities of grain could plausibly be military or intelligence agents mapping Egypt's strengths and reserves against a future attack. The charge of espionage — coming "to see the nakedness of the land" (לִרְאוֹת אֶת עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ, v. 9: lir'ot et ervat ha-arets) — has the specific meaning of identifying strategic vulnerabilities, exposed flanks, places where Egypt might be invaded or its resources seized. Ervat ha-arets ("nakedness of the land") is a military term for the undefended exposure of a territory.

Whether Joseph actually believed the accusation is debated by commentators. The narrative gives no indication that he did; his plan appears calculated from the beginning to bring Benjamin. The accusation is, formally, the pretext for detaining the brothers and imposing the test — a legal mechanism that serves Joseph's larger purpose. But it is also possible that Joseph needed information: he knows his brothers' character from bitter experience, and the accusation allows him to observe them under pressure before revealing himself.

The LXX renders meraglim as κατάσκοποι (kataskopin — "spies, scouts, those who watch/observe") — a direct equivalence. The formal translation renders "spies" throughout.

**5. "We are guilty" — אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ (ashemin anachnu) (v. 21)**

The brothers' confession in verse 21 is one of the most theologically significant sentences in the entire Joseph narrative: "Truly we are guilty concerning our brother, for we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us and we did not listen — therefore this distress has come upon us."

The word אֲשֵׁמִים (ashemin) is the plural adjective from the root א-שׁ-מ (a-sh-m — "to be guilty, to bear guilt, to be in a state of guilt before God or another"). The noun אָשָׁם (asham) is the technical term for the "guilt offering" in Leviticus 5–6 — the sacrifice required when one has committed a violation requiring reparation. The guilt-offering is not merely for acknowledged sin but for situations where one has wrongfully taken what belongs to another, where a debt of reparation is owed. The use of ashemin here connects the brothers' confession to this reparation-theology: they are not just sorry, they are guilty in the specific sense that requires restitution.

The confession is made in the third person, among themselves, not yet to Joseph. They are speaking in Hebrew to each other, unaware that Joseph understands (v. 23). This is the "private" confession — the inner acknowledgment of guilt that precedes the eventual public confession before Joseph (44:16, where Judah will explicitly confess: "God has found out the guilt — ha-asham — of your servants"). The arc from 42:21 to 44:16 traces the movement from private acknowledgment to public repentance.

The phrase אֶת צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ (et tsarat nafsho — "the distress of his soul") is the first retrospective narration of Joseph's captivity from the brothers' perspective. The verb חָנַן (chanan — "to plead, to implore for grace") in the relative clause — "when he pleaded with us" (בְּהִתְחַנְּנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ, b'hitchanneno eleinu) — describes Joseph's desperate appeals, of which nothing was recorded in the original selling scene (37:18–28). This is new narrative information: Joseph was crying out to them, begging for mercy, and they did not listen. The retrospective disclosure makes their guilt all the more vivid — what was omitted from chapter 37 is revealed here in the brothers' own confession.

The phrase "therefore this distress has come upon us" (עַל כֵּן בָּאָה אֵלֵינוּ הַצָּרָה הַזֹּאת — al ken ba'ah eleinu ha-tsarah ha-zot) uses the exact word צָרָה (tsarah — "distress, trouble") that was used for Joseph's condition (tsarat nafsho — "distress of his soul"). The brothers recognize the connection: the distress they did not relieve has now come back upon them in kind. This is not merely cosmic retribution language but explicit recognition of moral causation: we caused that distress; this distress has come because of that.

**6. "The interpreter was between them" — הַמֵּלִיץ בֵּינֹתָם (ha-melits beynotam) (v. 23)**

The narrative aside in verse 23 — "and they did not know that Joseph was listening, for the interpreter was between them" — is a remarkable window into the staging of the scene. The brothers are speaking in Hebrew to each other, apparently confident that the Egyptian viceroy before them cannot understand them. Joseph, of course, understands every word; he has been conducting the official interview in Egyptian through a מֵלִיץ (melits — "interpreter, translator, mediator").

The word מֵלִיץ (melits) is from the root ל-י-ץ (l-y-ts — "to mock, to make smooth, to interpret, to mediate"), the same root as the litsim ("mockers") in Proverbs and Job. In later Hebrew, laytsan/melits can mean both "interpreter" and "intercessor, advocate" — the one who stands between parties and smooths communication. Here the melits is simply the court interpreter who renders Joseph's Egyptian speech into Hebrew (or Canaanite) for the brothers and their Hebrew speech into Egyptian for the Egyptian court.

The irony is acute: the very instrument meant to create distance between Joseph and his brothers — the interpreter, marking Joseph as a foreign official — is the device that allows Joseph to hear their private confession. The melits stands between them officially, but Joseph is in fact inside their circle, hearing what they think is private speech. He has turned the institutional buffer of the royal interpreter into a tool of covert listening.

The LXX renders ha-melits as ὁ ἑρμηνευτής (ho hermeneutes — "the interpreter, the translator") — a direct equivalence. The formal translation renders "the interpreter."

**7. "And he wept" — וַיֵּבְךְּ (vayyevk) (v. 24)**

Joseph's weeping in verse 24 — "and he turned away from them and wept" — is the first of four weeping scenes in the remainder of the Joseph narrative:

- **42:24**: Turns away and weeps alone, after hearing the brothers' confession
- **43:30**: Goes into a private room and weeps, when he sees Benjamin
- **45:2**: Weeps loudly at his self-revelation, heard by all Egypt
- **50:1**: Falls on his father's face and weeps, at Jacob's death

The weeping in 42:24 is the most controlled and private: Joseph turns away (וַיִּסֹּב — vayyissov) before weeping, concealing the emotion. He cannot yet reveal himself — the test is not complete, Benjamin has not come, the second dream has not been fulfilled. But the brothers' confession has broken something loose in him. Their words (v. 21) — "we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us" — are the first acknowledgment that Joseph's suffering was real and was witnessed. He is hearing, for the first time, that they knew what they were doing, and that they have carried this guilt for years.

The weeping is brief: he returns (vayyashov), speaks again, and proceeds with the plan. The emotion is there and real; it is mastered and concealed. This pattern — emotion acknowledged, then controlled, for the sake of the larger purpose — characterizes Joseph throughout these chapters. He knows something his brothers do not know, and he cannot let that knowledge escape until the right moment.

The verb וַיֵּבְךְּ (vayyevk) is Qal imperfect with waw-consecutive from ב-כ-ה (b-k-h — "to weep"), used consistently for Joseph's four weeping scenes. The simplicity of the single-verb notation — "and he wept" — gives the act the plainness of controlled disclosure: the narrator records it without elaboration.

**8. "A bundle of silver" — צְרוֹר כַּסְפּוֹ (tsror kaspo) (vv. 27, 28, 35)**

The discovery of the silver is rendered in two slightly different forms: in verse 27 a single man finds his כֶּסֶף (keseph — "silver, money") in his שַׂק (sak — "bag, sack"), described as being "in the mouth of his bag" (פִּי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ — pi amtachto). In verse 35, the same discovery is made at home: each man's צְרוֹר כַּסְפּוֹ (tsror kaspo — "bundle/packet of silver") is in his sack (אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ — amtachto).

The word צְרוֹר (tsror) means "bundle, packet, pouch" — a tied or bound collection of small items, here the tied purse in which silver coins or weights would be kept. The image is of a little pouch or bag of silver that has inexplicably reappeared in the grain sack: the men paid for grain and took their sacks home, and now the payment has come back with them. They did not take it — it was put there by someone else (Joseph, v. 25: "restore each man's silver to his sack"). This unexplained return of payment transforms what was a commercial transaction into something ominous and portentous: grain purchased but silver not received, an unexplained gift from an unknown source in a foreign land.

The brothers' question — "What is this that God has done to us?" (v. 28) — marks the returned silver as a divine act in their interpretation. They do not know whether it is gracious or threatening, but they recognize it as outside the normal order. The fact that Joseph commanded the return of the silver (v. 25) is hidden from them; they know only that they paid and now the payment has come back. The silver will be found a second time in chapter 43 (vv. 12, 21), and its return will be the occasion for further anxious explanation to the Egyptian official. Joseph uses the returned silver as an ongoing mystery that keeps the brothers off-balance throughout the negotiation.

**9. "Jacob their father said to them: You have bereaved me" — כּוּלָּם הָיוּ עָלַי (kullam hayu alai) (v. 36)**

Jacob's lament in verse 36 is one of the most emotionally concentrated speeches in Genesis: "You have bereaved me — Joseph is no more, and Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin! Upon me have all these things come."

The opening verb — שִׁכַּלְתֶּם אֹתִי (shikkaltem oti — "you have bereaved me") — is the Piel of שׁ-כ-ל (sh-k-l — "to bereave, to be childless through the loss of children, to suffer the loss of offspring"). The Piel is causative/factitive: "you have made me childless." The root is associated specifically with the loss of children through death or violent removal — it is the most specific term for parental grief over lost children. Rachael used the related noun שַׁכּוּל (shakkul — "bereaved mother") in 43:14 for the anguish of a mother bereft of children. Jacob's use of the verb here accuses the brothers directly: they are the agents of his bereavement.

The accusation is spoken in anguish rather than with full knowledge: Jacob does not know how Joseph was lost. He believes (since Genesis 37:33) that "Joseph is no more" — using the same phrase the brothers will use in their report (42:13: "the one is no more"). Now Simeon is detained in Egypt. Now they want to take Benjamin. The three absences are stacked: Joseph / Simeon / Benjamin — one dead (as Jacob believes), one hostage, one threatened. In Jacob's experience, sending his sons away means losing them.

The final phrase — עָלַי הָיוּ כֻלָּנָה (alai hayu kullana — "upon me have all these things come") — is Jacob's sense of cumulative and personal weight: all of this loss falls upon him. The phrase is syntactically emphatic: the prepositional phrase "upon me" precedes the verb and subject, frontloading the sense of personal burden. The chapter that began with Jacob's practical assessment — "there is grain in Egypt, go down" — ends with Jacob's grief-cry: upon me, all of this.

**10. "Bring down my gray head in sorrow to Sheol" — לְהוֹרִיד שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאוֹלָה (l'horid seyvati b'yagon she'olah) (v. 38)**

The chapter's closing line — Jacob's refusal to send Benjamin, grounded in the fear of "bringing my gray head down in sorrow to Sheol" — establishes the terms of Jacob's grief and the stakes of the test Joseph has set.

**שֵׂיבָה (seyvah — "gray head, gray hair, old age")** is the term for the distinguished white/gray hair of old age, a sign of long life and dignified eldership in Hebrew culture. The "gray head" is the synecdoche for Jacob's aged person — his accumulated years and dignity, all that he has lived and suffered. To "bring down the gray head to Sheol" is to destroy the aged person, to kill the old man through grief.

**יָגוֹן (yagon — "sorrow, grief, anguish")** is the preposition-noun phrase that specifies the mode of death: not merely dying, but dying in active grief, with sorrow. Jacob is not merely contemplating death; he is contemplating a death shaped entirely by accumulated loss. The phrase be-yagon ("in sorrow") is the keynote: whatever happens to Jacob, he has framed it through the lens of grief. He has been grieving since Genesis 37:34–35, and Genesis 42 ends with him still in that posture — "I will go down to my son in mourning to Sheol" (37:35). The phrase here echoes and extends that earlier statement.

**שְׁאוֹל (Sheol)** is the Hebrew term for the realm of the dead — the underworld where the shades (rephaim) of the dead continue a shadowy existence. It is not explicitly a place of punishment in the earlier strata of the Old Testament but simply the destination of the dead, the place to which all people descend. The directional suffix -ah (she'olah — "to Sheol, Sheol-ward") is the standard way of indicating direction or destination. Jacob uses Sheol twice in the Joseph narrative — here and in 37:35 — both times as the place where he expects to join his (apparently) dead son Joseph. The echo is stark: the chapter 37 lament ("I will go down to Sheol in mourning for my son") is reprised at the end of chapter 42, as Jacob still believes Joseph is dead and now fears losing Benjamin too.

The LXX renders Sheol as ᾅδης (Hades — the Greek underworld of the dead), its standard rendering throughout the OT. The formal translation preserves "Sheol" as the Hebrew term, consistent with the established convention.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 42

Pages 42–43 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text of Vaticanus was legible throughout both pages, with some surface wear and spotting on page 42 but with the core text recoverable. Page 43 carries the header "CAP XLVIII" in the upper margin, reflecting the Vaticanus chapter enumeration. Key variants and equivalences observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 42:1 | וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב ("and Jacob saw") | ἰδὼν δὲ Ιακωβ ("and Jacob, having seen") | MT followed | The LXX uses a participial construction (ἰδών — "having seen") where MT has the standard wayyiqtol chain. This is a characteristic LXX tendency toward participial subordination. |
| 42:1 | שֶׁבֶר ("grain") | σῖτος ("grain, wheat") | MT followed | MT shever ("broken/commercial grain") is rendered with the standard Greek grain term. The formal translation renders "grain" throughout as the functional English equivalent. |
| 42:6 | וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה ("and they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground") | προσεκύνησαν αὐτῷ ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ("they did obeisance to him upon their face upon the ground") | Equivalent | MT: vayyishtachavu lo appayim artsah. LXX: prosekunesan autō epi prosopon epi tes ges. Both preserve the full physical act of prostration — faces to the ground. The LXX's epi prosopon is a more analytical rendering of the MT's idiom (appayim — "nostrils/faces" as synecdoche for the whole face). |
| 42:7 | וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם ("he made himself unrecognizable to them") | ἠλλοτριοῦτο ἀπ' αὐτῶν ("he was alienating himself from them") | MT followed | MT: vayyitnakker (Hithpael of n-k-r — "made himself strange/unrecognizable"). LXX: ēllotriuto (imperfect of ἀλλοτριόω — "to alienate, to make foreign/strange"). The LXX's rendering emphasizes the social/relational estrangement while the MT emphasizes the concealment of identity. The formal translation follows MT: "made himself unrecognizable." |
| 42:9 | לִרְאוֹת אֶת עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ ("to see the nakedness of the land") | ἰδεῖν τὰ ἴχνη τῆς χώρας ("to see the tracks/traces of the land") | MT followed | MT: lir'ot et ervat ha-arets ("to see the nakedness/exposed vulnerability of the land"). LXX: idein ta ichne tes choras ("to see the footprints/tracks of the region") — a significantly different military metaphor (tracking the movements of the land rather than seeing its exposed weakness). The MT's military term "nakedness" is the stronger image and is followed. |
| 42:15 | בְּחֵי פַרְעֹה ("by the life of Pharaoh") | νὴ τὴν ὑγίειαν Φαραω ("by the health of Pharaoh") | MT followed | MT: be-chei Phar'oh ("by the life of Pharaoh") — an Egyptian oath formula using חַי (chai — "life"). LXX: ne ten hygieian Pharao ("by the health of Pharaoh") — rendering the life-oath with a slightly different Greek formula emphasizing physical vitality. Both are oath formulas; the formal translation follows MT: "by the life of Pharaoh." |
| 42:21 | אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ ("we are guilty") | ἔνοχοί ἐσμεν ("we are liable/guilty") | Equivalent | MT: ashemin anachnu. LXX: enochoi esmen. Both affirm guilt/culpability. The Greek ἔνοχος ("guilty, liable, subject to") is from the root ἔχω ("to hold"), implying being held/bound by guilt — compatible with the asham reparation-concept of the MT. The formal translation renders "we are guilty." |
| 42:21 | צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ ("the distress of his soul") | τὴν θλῖψιν τῆς ψυχῆς αὐτοῦ ("the affliction of his soul") | Equivalent | MT: tsarat nafsho. LXX: ten thlipsin tes psyches autou. Both capture the inner anguish of Joseph's soul in the pit. Greek θλῖψις ("affliction, pressure, tribulation") is slightly more intensive than the MT's tsarah but is semantically compatible. |
| 42:23 | הַמֵּלִיץ בֵּינֹתָם ("the interpreter between them") | ὁ ἑρμηνευτής ("the interpreter") | Equivalent | MT: ha-melits beynotam ("the interpreter between them"). LXX: ho hermeneutes — the standard Greek term for an interpreter or translator. Direct equivalence; the formal translation renders "the interpreter." |
| 42:24 | וַיִּסֹּב מֵעֲלֵיהֶם וַיֵּבְךְּ ("and he turned away from them and wept") | ἀποστραφεὶς δὲ ἀπ' αὐτῶν ἔκλαυσεν ("and turning away from them he wept") | Equivalent | Both MT and LXX record the same sequence of motion and emotion: turning away, weeping. The LXX uses a participial construction for the turn (ἀποστραφείς) followed by the main verb of weeping (ἔκλαυσεν). |
| 42:36 | שִׁכַּלְתֶּם אֹתִי ("you have bereaved me") | ἄτεκνόν με ἐποιήσατε ("you have made me childless") | Equivalent | MT: shikkaltem oti (Piel of sh-k-l — "to bereave of children"). LXX: ateknon me epoiesate ("you have made me childless"). Both express the specific grief of parental loss. The Greek ἄτεκνος ("childless") is from α- ("without") + τέκνον ("child"), directly matching the root meaning of sh-k-l. |
| 42:38 | שְׁאוֹלָה ("to Sheol") | εἰς ᾅδου ("to Hades") | MT followed | MT: she'olah ("to Sheol," with directional he). LXX: eis hadou ("to Hades/the underworld"). The formal translation preserves "Sheol" per established convention. |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 42**

Genesis 42 is architecturally a chapter of reversal and preparation: reversals of power and knowledge, preparations for the fuller reversals and recognitions that will come in chapters 43–45. The structure is carefully framed:

- **Vv. 1–5**: Jacob's initiative — the famine compels action; ten brothers sent, Benjamin withheld
- **Vv. 6–9**: The prostration — dream partially fulfilled; Joseph recognizes but withholds; accuses of espionage
- **Vv. 10–17**: First confrontation — brothers defend themselves; Joseph imposes the test; three days' custody
- **Vv. 18–20**: Revised test — one stays, nine return for grain, must bring Benjamin
- **Vv. 21–24**: Private confession — brothers' guilt acknowledged; Joseph weeps privately; Simeon bound
- **Vv. 25–28**: Silver returned — the mysterious gift that creates anxiety on the road
- **Vv. 29–34**: Brothers' report to Jacob — the test conditions named
- **Vv. 35–38**: Jacob's lament — silver found, fear, bereavement-cry, refusal to release Benjamin

The chapter is organized around the theme of knowledge asymmetry: Joseph knows everything (who the brothers are, what they did, what the dreams meant), while the brothers know nothing (that the viceroy is Joseph, that their confession was heard, why the silver was returned). This asymmetry of knowledge is the structural engine of the chapter: everything that happens is shaped by what one party knows that the other does not.

**The Dream-Fulfillment Thread**

Genesis 37:5–11 recorded two dreams. The first dream (37:6–7): eleven sheaves bowing to Joseph's sheaf. The second dream (37:9): eleven stars, along with the sun and moon, bowing to Joseph. The first dream concerns the brothers; the second concerns the whole family including the parents.

Genesis 42:6 fulfills the first dream in its initial form: ten brothers bow. But the fulfillment is incomplete — the dream had eleven sheaves. The ten-brothers visit of chapter 42 cannot complete the first dream because Benjamin is absent. Joseph's entire test is designed to bring the eleven together. The second dream (the sun, moon, and eleven stars) awaits Genesis 46 and 47 when Jacob himself descends to Egypt.

This structure suggests that Joseph's test is not arbitrary or merely motivated by desire for information — it is shaped by the logic of the dreams' fulfillment. Until Benjamin comes, the first dream remains uncompleted. Joseph is, at some level, the agent of the dreams' full realization: his test will produce the eleven, and the eleven bowing before him (43:26, 28) will complete the first dream. The second dream awaits Jacob.

**The Confession as Turning Point**

The confession of 42:21 — "we are guilty concerning our brother" — marks the narrative turning point of the entire Joseph story. The brothers' sin in Genesis 37 had never been acknowledged within the narrative until this moment. They sold Joseph, deceived their father, and resumed life. The guilt was never confronted.

The crisis of Egypt forces the confrontation. Faced with an Egyptian official who treats them as criminals, they interpret their predicament as divine retribution — "therefore this distress has come upon us." The connection between their past action and present situation is made explicitly by Reuben's retrospective testimony (v. 22), who recalls his own failed opposition: "Did I not say to you, 'Do not sin against the boy'?" Reuben's speech adds the dimension of prophecy-fulfilled: he warned them then; now the consequence has come. The structure of moral causation — guilt produces consequence — is acknowledged by the very men who created the guilt.

This is the beginning of the brothers' repentance arc that will culminate in 44:16 (Judah's full confession) and 45:4–13 (Joseph's self-revelation and its reception). Genesis 42:21–22 is the narrative seed of the full repentance that will bloom in Genesis 44–45.

**Jacob's Grief as Narrative Frame**

Jacob's grief over Joseph (37:34–35) opened the post-betrayal phase of the Joseph narrative. His grief-refusal in 42:36–38 closes chapter 42 in the same posture. The chapter is framed by Jacob's active role — he sends his sons (vv. 1–2) and he refuses to release Benjamin (vv. 37–38) — but between those acts of agency is a man defined by loss. The bereavement-cry "Joseph is no more, Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin!" reduces Jacob's world to the serial disappearance of his children.

The irony the narrator maintains throughout: the reader knows that Joseph is not dead. Jacob's grief is real but based on false information. The dramatic irony is sustained through chapters 42–45 precisely to make the revelation of chapter 45 — "I am Joseph; is my father still alive?" — the more overwhelming. Jacob's every expression of grief is a testimony to what he does not yet know. The Sheol-saying of verse 38 will be recontextualized entirely in chapter 45 when he learns that the son he thought was in Sheol is instead the viceroy of Egypt.
